title: 7.8 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 7.8!
topic_path: The Long Nineteenth Century (1750 to 1914 CE)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why was the slaving system *economically* damaging for middle-class British industrialists and business owners?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It created unfair competition with businesses using unpaid enslaved labor.
Many believed that slavery was immoral and violated Christian values.
It caused labor shortages in Britain’s factories by sending workers to colonies.
It violated religious doctrine, making it unpopular with the Church.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Enlightenment thinking contribute to the abolition of slavery?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It sparked new debates about universal human rights and equality.
It encouraged the middle-class to study the fine arts.
It emphasized the separation of church and state.
It caused literacy rates to decline among working-class people.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did enslaved or formerly enslaved people contribute to the abolition of slavery?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They wrote and spoke publicly about their experiences to sway public opinion.
They led rebellions and revolutions that directly challenged slaveholding powers.
They negotiated with slave owners to reduce the working hours on plantations.
They helped European governments create new tax systems to make slavery less profitable.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the shift from farming to industry impact the need for child labor?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It increased demand for child labor because factories needed cheap, unskilled workers.
It decreased the need for child labor because machines replaced human workers.
It had no effect on child labor, which remained constant from earlier farming societies.
It improved working conditions for children through government safety regulations.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What best describes the working conditions for child laborers in early industrial factories?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They worked long hours in dangerous environments with little protection.
They received fair wages and access to education through factory-sponsored programs.
They were mostly supervised by family members and worked only part-time.
They were protected by strong labor laws that limited working hours and improved safety.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best identifies an economic argument used against child labor during industrialization?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Child labor lowered wages and reduced job opportunities for adult workers.
Child labor boosted the economy by creating more consumers at a younger age.
Hiring children improved factory efficiency and encouraged innovation.
Children were more likely to unionize and demand higher wages.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains why the global fight for women’s suffrage appears inconsistent across countries and time periods?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Different nations granted women voting rights at different times based on their unique situations.
Most women around the world were not interested in gaining the right to vote until after World War II.
Women were universally granted voting rights after the formation of the United Nations.
The disjointed nature of the movement shows that women’s suffrage was only important in Western democracies.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What were common arguments made against granting women the right to vote during the 19th and early 20th centuries?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Critics claimed that women lacked the knowledge needed to vote responsibly.
Opponents argued that women’s suffrage would disrupt traditional family roles and values.
Governments supported suffrage but couldn’t implement it due to budget constraints.
Most women chose not to vote, so there was no need to expand suffrage.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why were women’s suffrage movements in Latin America and Africa slower to develop than in other parts of the world?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Colonial rule and political instability limited opportunities for organizing around women's rights.
Women in these regions were not interested in participating in politics.
Universal suffrage was already granted to all citizens in these areas.
Cultural traditions strictly prohibited women from engaging in public life.

assessment_items.question_content:
The following excerpt is from a testimony by Matthew Crabtree (1832), a former child laborer in a textile mill in England:

>*At what time in the morning did you begin to work?*  
—At six o’clock in the morning.  
*What time did you leave off?*  
—At eight o’clock at night.  
*Were you excessively fatigued by that length of labor?*  
—Yes, we were very much fatigued. I have felt myself so fatigued that I could scarcely preserve power to eat my dinner.

**Which of the following best explains how testimonies like this supported the movement for child labor reform?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They exposed the harsh realities of factory work and swayed public opinion toward legal protections.
They proved that children were the most productive factory workers.
They emphasized the benefits of industrial work for building children's strength and endurance.
They encouraged children to demand better working conditions through organized strikes.

assessment_items.question_content:
The image below was used by abolitionists to appeal to the public’s conscience and argue against the use of enslaved labor.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which explanation for the end of slavery is most clearly supported by this image?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A kneeling black man in chains looks upward in a posture of pleading or prayer. The words “Am I Not a Man and a Brother?” are written around him in bold letters.
options.caption: “*Am I Not a Man and a Brother?*” by the American Anti-Slavery Society (1837), public domain.

choices.content: The end of slavery was driven by the growing belief in shared humanity and moral responsibility.
The end of slavery was caused by the economic pressures created by industrial capitalism.
The end of slavery resulted from the fear of violent rebellions and uprisings.
The end of slavery came from the increasing costs of maintaining overseas colonies.

assessment_items.question_content:
>I do not wish women to have power over men, but over themselves. 

>— Mary Wollstonecraft, *A Vindication of the Rights of Woman,* 1792


**What does this passage suggest about Enlightenment principles and the case for women’s suffrage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It argues that women deserve the right to vote to assert control over their own interests.
It endorses women having authority over men and reversing traditional power dynamics.
It suggests that women should focus on domestic duties rather than political rights.
It claims women already had sufficient rights but lacked awareness of them.
